title: Why Smart People Feel Like Frauds: The Psychology of Impostor Syndrome and Its Hidden Benefits
description:
in Life, Psychology |   	March 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Incom¬≠pe¬≠tent peo¬≠ple tend to see them¬≠selves as not just com¬≠pe¬≠tent, but high
body:
in Life, Psychology |   	March 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Incom¬≠pe¬≠tent peo¬≠ple tend to see them¬≠selves as not just com¬≠pe¬≠tent, but high¬≠ly com¬≠pe¬≠tent. So, at any rate, holds the the¬≠o¬≠ry of the ‚ÄúDun¬≠ning-Kruger effect,‚Äù pre¬≠vi¬≠ous¬≠ly fea¬≠tured here on Open Cul¬≠ture. But does the con¬≠verse also hold: do high¬≠ly com¬≠pe¬≠tent peo¬≠ple tend to see them¬≠selves as incom¬≠pe¬≠tent? That would seem to be an impli¬≠ca¬≠tion of what‚Äôs been called ‚Äúimpos¬≠tor syn¬≠drome,‚Äù a per¬≠sis¬≠tent sense of inad¬≠e¬≠qua¬≠cy rel¬≠a¬≠tive to one‚Äôs sta¬≠tus or posi¬≠tion, unsup¬≠port¬≠ed by any objec¬≠tive evi¬≠dence. If you your¬≠self have been afflict¬≠ed with that con¬≠di¬≠tion, it may be a tad hasty to take it as a sign of your own effec¬≠tive¬≠ness, but as the Har¬≠vard Busi¬≠ness School‚Äôs Arthur C. Brooks explains in the clip above, it may nonethe¬≠less ben¬≠e¬≠fit you to lean into it.
‚ÄúWhat all strivers I‚Äôve ever met have in com¬≠mon is that, the high¬≠er they climb and the more suc¬≠cess they have, the more inse¬≠cure they feel in their own suc¬≠cess because they‚Äôre not quite sure that they‚Äôve earned it or deserve it,‚Äù Brooks says. Iron¬≠i¬≠cal¬≠ly, in his expe¬≠ri¬≠ence, ‚Äúpeo¬≠ple who deserve suc¬≠cess through hard work and mer¬≠it and per¬≠son¬≠al respon¬≠si¬≠bil¬≠i¬≠ty are not quite sure they deserve it, and the peo¬≠ple who don‚Äôt deserve it are often the peo¬≠ple who are actu¬≠al¬≠ly most sure that they do.‚Äù
In that last group are pos¬≠ses¬≠sors of the so-called ‚Äúdark tri¬≠ad‚Äù traits: nar¬≠cis¬≠sism, Machi¬≠avel¬≠lian¬≠ism, and psy¬≠chopa¬≠thy. A ‚Äúgood, nor¬≠mal, healthy per¬≠son,‚Äù by con¬≠trast, will nat¬≠u¬≠ral¬≠ly won¬≠der if they real¬≠ly mer¬≠it the pro¬≠mo¬≠tions, rewards, and acco¬≠lades they receive, and if they‚Äôre tru¬≠ly up to each task ahead.
To com¬≠bat impos¬≠tor syn¬≠drome, Brooks rec¬≠om¬≠mends you¬Ý‚Äúunder¬≠stand it, keep up to date with it, and keep try¬≠ing to get bet¬≠ter at the things you‚Äôre not good at yet.‚Äù Human¬≠i¬≠ty‚Äôs gen¬≠er¬≠al neg¬≠a¬≠tiv¬≠i¬≠ty bias may keep most of us sus¬≠pect¬≠ing that we‚Äôve been over¬≠es¬≠ti¬≠mat¬≠ed, but that does¬≠n‚Äôt mean we should ignore the oppor¬≠tu¬≠ni¬≠ties for gen¬≠uine self-improve¬≠ment that such feel¬≠ings present. ‚ÄúThe truth is, if things are real¬≠ly, real¬≠ly rough for you, you‚Äôre not all weak¬≠ness¬≠es, and if things are going real¬≠ly well for you, you‚Äôre not all strengths.‚Äù It just hap¬≠pens that at some times, every¬≠one focus¬≠es on the for¬≠mer, and at oth¬≠er times, the lat¬≠ter, and what‚Äôs impor¬≠tant is not to let your¬≠self be too heav¬≠i¬≠ly influ¬≠enced in either case. Per¬≠haps you can stay ground¬≠ed by bear¬≠ing in mind a cou¬≠ple of¬Ýtrusty old adages: that nobody‚Äôs per¬≠fect, and that you do, some¬≠times, have to fake it ‚Äôtil you make it.
Relat¬≠ed con¬≠tent:
24 Com¬≠mon Cog¬≠ni¬≠tive Bias¬≠es: A Visu¬≠al List of the Psy¬≠cho¬≠log¬≠i¬≠cal Sys¬≠tems Errors That Keep Us From Think¬≠ing Ratio¬≠nal¬≠ly
Why Incom¬≠pe¬≠tent Peo¬≠ple Think They‚Äôre Com¬≠pe¬≠tent: The Dun¬≠ning-Kruger Effect, Explained
Why Over¬≠con¬≠fi¬≠dence Is Our Most Dan¬≠ger¬≠ous Cog¬≠ni¬≠tive Bias
John Cleese on How ‚ÄúStu¬≠pid Peo¬≠ple Have No Idea How Stu¬≠pid They Are‚Äù (Oth¬≠er¬≠wise Known as the Dun¬≠ning-Kruger Effect)
There Are Eight Forms of Intel¬≠li¬≠gence, Not Just One: Which Apply to You?
The Sur¬≠pris¬≠ing Pow¬≠er of Bore¬≠dom: It Lets You Con¬≠front Big Ques¬≠tions & Give Life Mean¬≠ing
Based in Seoul,¬ÝCol¬≠in¬ÝMarshall¬Ýwrites and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬ÝBooks on Cities¬Ýas well as the books¬ÝÌïúÍµ≠ ÏöîÏïΩ Í∏àÏßÄ¬Ý(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬ÝKore¬≠an Newtro.¬ÝFol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬Ý@colinmarshall.

title: Every Known Work by Georgia O‚ÄôKeeffe Has Been Digitized and Made Available Online
description:
in Archives, Art, Museums |   	March 23rd, 2026  1 Comment
	
			
Upon hear¬≠ing the names of Arthur Dove or Mars¬≠den Hart¬≠ley, the sat¬≠u¬≠rat¬≠ed col¬≠o
body:
in Archives, Art, Museums |   	March 23rd, 2026  1 Comment
	
			
Upon hear¬≠ing the names of Arthur Dove or Mars¬≠den Hart¬≠ley, the sat¬≠u¬≠rat¬≠ed col¬≠ors and organ¬≠i¬≠cal¬≠ly askew lines of those painters‚Äô land¬≠scapes may appear before your mind‚Äôs eye. But unless you have a spe¬≠cial inter¬≠est in Amer¬≠i¬≠can mod¬≠ernists of the ear¬≠ly twen¬≠ti¬≠eth cen¬≠tu¬≠ry, they prob¬≠a¬≠bly don‚Äôt. The name Geor¬≠gia O‚ÄôKe¬≠effe, by con¬≠trast, can hard¬≠ly fail to bring a few images even to the mind of the strict¬≠ly casu¬≠al art appre¬≠ci¬≠a¬≠tor: New Mex¬≠i¬≠can mesas, ani¬≠mal skulls, and above all flow¬≠ers in extreme close-up. Apart from the artis¬≠tic skill and dis¬≠tinc¬≠tive vision with which she cre¬≠at¬≠ed it, O‚ÄôKe¬≠ef¬≠fe‚Äôs work per¬≠sists in the wider cul¬≠ture because of how well it hap¬≠pens to repro¬≠duce in a vari¬≠ety of con¬≠texts, includ¬≠ing post¬≠cards, mugs, and even appar¬≠el, such as that sold at her epony¬≠mous muse¬≠um in San¬≠ta Fe.

Keep¬≠ing such prod¬≠ucts around is, of course, no sub¬≠sti¬≠tute for see¬≠ing the real thing; in their phys¬≠i¬≠cal real¬≠i¬≠ty, O‚ÄôKe¬≠ef¬≠fe‚Äôs paint¬≠ings have a way of rebuff¬≠ing all the inter¬≠pre¬≠ta¬≠tions with which they‚Äôve been freight¬≠ed for more than a cen¬≠tu¬≠ry now. If you can‚Äôt make it out to New Mex¬≠i¬≠co, the Geor¬≠gia O‚ÄôKe¬≠effe Muse¬≠um has been work¬≠ing to make every sin¬≠gle one of her pieces (includ¬≠ing sculp¬≠tures and pho¬≠tographs) avail¬≠able for view¬≠ing online at a just-launched por¬≠tal called Access O‚ÄôKe¬≠effe.
The muse¬≠um describes it as a ‚Äúuser-friend¬≠ly, search¬≠able web¬≠site with high-res¬≠o¬≠lu¬≠tion images, visu¬≠al descrip¬≠tions, exhi¬≠bi¬≠tion his¬≠to¬≠ries, archival mate¬≠ri¬≠als, and research data asso¬≠ci¬≠at¬≠ed with the artist‚Äôs two-vol¬≠ume cat¬≠a¬≠logue raison¬≠n√©.‚Äù The site‚Äôs vis¬≠i¬≠tors ‚Äúcan browse by col¬≠or, shape, or medi¬≠um, explore the con¬≠text of works cre¬≠at¬≠ed before and after a spe¬≠cif¬≠ic paint¬≠ing, trace his¬≠toric exhi¬≠bi¬≠tions, cre¬≠ate lists of favorites, and down¬≠load images.‚Äù

Access O‚ÄôKe¬≠effe makes it easy to find the artist‚Äôs most famous paint¬≠ings, but also works that may sur¬≠prise view¬≠ers who only know her mesas, skulls, and flow¬≠ers. Take, for exam¬≠ple, such noc¬≠tur¬≠nal¬≠ly themed can¬≠vas¬≠es as her ear¬≠ly Starlight Night, from 1917, or her late Unti¬≠tled (City Night), from the nine¬≠teen-sev¬≠en¬≠ties. O‚ÄôKe¬≠ef¬≠fe‚Äôs Amer¬≠i¬≠ca, we must remem¬≠ber, isn‚Äôt lim¬≠it¬≠ed to the desert: though she did spend most of her near¬≠ly cen¬≠tu¬≠ry-long life‚Äôs sec¬≠ond half in New Mex¬≠i¬≠co, it¬Ýalso took her from Wis¬≠con¬≠sin to Vir¬≠ginia to Texas to New York, with stints in South Car¬≠oli¬≠na and Hawaii. Giv¬≠en the impor¬≠tance of under¬≠stand¬≠ing any artist‚Äôs con¬≠texts both geo¬≠graph¬≠i¬≠cal and social,¬ÝAccess O‚ÄôKe¬≠effe also pro¬≠vides an archive of arti¬≠facts and exhi¬≠bi¬≠tions relat¬≠ed to the peo¬≠ple and orga¬≠ni¬≠za¬≠tions asso¬≠ci¬≠at¬≠ed with her¬Ý‚Äî Arthur Dove and Mars¬≠den Hart¬≠ley includ¬≠ed.
Relat¬≠ed con¬≠tent:
Geor¬≠gia O‚ÄôKeeffe: A Life in Art, a Short Doc¬≠u¬≠men¬≠tary on the Painter Nar¬≠rat¬≠ed by Gene Hack¬≠man
How Geor¬≠gia O‚ÄôKeeffe Became Geor¬≠gia O‚ÄôKeeffe: An Ani¬≠mat¬≠ed Video Tells the Sto¬≠ry
An Intro¬≠duc¬≠tion to the Paint¬≠ing That Changed Geor¬≠gia O‚ÄôKeeffe‚Äôs Career: Ram‚Äôs Head, White Hol¬≠ly¬≠hock-Hills
The Real Geor¬≠gia O‚ÄôKeeffe: The Artist Reveals Her¬≠self in Vin¬≠tage Doc¬≠u¬≠men¬≠tary Clips
Recipes from the Kitchen of Geor¬≠gia O‚ÄôKeeffe
Alfred Stieglitz: The Elo¬≠quent Eye, a Reveal¬≠ing Look at ‚ÄúThe Father of Mod¬≠ern Pho¬≠tog¬≠ra¬≠phy‚Äù
Based in Seoul,¬ÝCol¬≠in¬ÝMarshall¬Ýwrites and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬ÝBooks on Cities¬Ýas well as the books¬ÝÌïúÍµ≠ ÏöîÏïΩ Í∏àÏßÄ¬Ý(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬ÝKore¬≠an Newtro.¬ÝFol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬Ý@colinmarshall.

title: Meditate to the Undulations of Baltic Sea Ice in Jan Erik Waider's Hypnotic Videos
description: Jan Erik Waider has a knack for capturing shorelines, volcanic eruptions, and glaciers at their most mesmerizing‚Äîshrouded in mist, glowing in the
body:
Jan Erik Waider has a knack for capturing shorelines, volcanic eruptions, and glaciers at their most mesmerizing‚Äîshrouded in mist, glowing in the darkness, or illuminated by pale northern light. His atmospheric photographs of icy seas and rugged landscapes from Iceland to the Antarctic, focus on dramatic forms and cast remote places into a dreamy ethereality.
Most recently, Waider captured a striking phenomenon in the Baltic Sea, just off the coast of northern Germany. Fresh ice formed a thin layer on the rolling surface, creating faceted, polygon-like shapes that moved gently and rhythmically with the waves without breaking apart.



Waider‚Äôs aerial drone perspective creates an otherworldly, almost totally abstract effect. At first glance, it appears as though it could be a minimalist animation highlighting the interactions between water, light, and motion. ‚ÄúSoft evening light, fine crack lines, and shifting tones from warm gold to deep green turned this fleeting moment into a study of structure, depth, and calm,‚Äù Waider says.
See more on Waider‚Äôs YouTube channel, Instagram, and Behance.

schema.headline: Meditate to the Undulations of Baltic Sea Ice in Jan Erik Waider's Hypnotic Videos

title: How Do Artists Finance Their Lives? Join Us for a Discussion About Mason Currey's New Book
description: As with most conversations about money, understanding how artists fund their practices and lives is rarely discussed and always of intrigue. Mason
body:
As with most conversations about money, understanding how artists fund their practices and lives is rarely discussed and always of intrigue. Mason Currey¬Ýdives into this underexplored topic in his new book Making Art and Making a Living: Adventures in Funding a Creative Life. 
Currey is known for documenting the day-to-day routines of hundreds of artists, writers, filmmakers, designers, musicians, and more. Making Art and Making a Living is in the same vein, revealing how family money, day jobs, schemes, and more have buoyed artists through the ages.
‚ÄãIn partnership with EXPO Chicago and the Chicago Athletic Association, we‚Äôre thrilled to celebrate the launch of Currey‚Äôs new book. Join us on April 8 for a discussion and signing by the author. Tickets include a hardcover copy, and Colossal Members¬Ýreceive $10 off with the code in their account.
Find Making Art and Making a Living in the Colossal Shop, and register for the event to save your seat.

March 22, 2026
ArtCraft
Kate Mothes

If it weren‚Äôt for being so li
body:
‚ÄúArtist‚Äôs Palette Vase.‚Äù All images courtesy of the artist, shared with permission
March 22, 2026
ArtCraft
Kate Mothes

If it weren‚Äôt for being so lightweight and crisp in their facets, Goran Konjevod‚Äôs elegant vases could at first glance be mistaken for thin porcelain. Crafted instead from precisely folded paper, the works tap into the relationship between‚Äîand associations with‚Äîmaterial, form, and function. His meticulous origami compositions combine organic forms with nuanced hues and gradients, creating a sense of visual heft and presence from thin, gauzy material.
Konjevod‚Äôs work was recently included in Art of the Fold at ACCI Gallery, and ‚ÄúGrey Curves Vase‚Äù and ‚ÄúArtist‚Äôs Palette Vase‚Äù will be part of an exhibition titled The Craft of Paper: Contemporary Takes on Tradition this August at the Robert C. Williams Museum of Papermaking in Atlanta. See more on Instagram.

title: Stephen Morrison's Trompe-L'≈ìil 'Dog World' Paintings Are Fetching
description: Any dog owner can appreciate the kind of unfettered, often visceral reactions canines have to everything from their favorite treats to a scurrying
body:
Any dog owner can appreciate the kind of unfettered, often visceral reactions canines have to everything from their favorite treats to a scurrying squirrel to another dog passing by the window. Their lack of inhibition and legendary fidelity bring comfort, routine, and goofiness to our daily lives despite their total unawareness of their effects on us. For Stephen Morrison, curiosity and play find their way into vibrant, quirky paintings that ‚Äúinvite viewers to rediscover the magic and absurdity often obscured by the routine,‚Äù he says.
Morrison‚Äôs practice has lately revolved around trompe l‚Äô≈ìil compositions of everyday objects and tableaux in which dogs‚Äô features appear unexpectedly. A snout stands in for the flap of a handbag or juts out from the side of a Pepsi can. His current solo exhibition, Dog Show #5: Field Recordings at SLAG&RX, centers on a series of objects referencing places he worked on the pieces‚ÄîParis, New York City, and Maine‚Äîthat also play important roles in his life.
‚Äú111 Limerock Street‚Äù (2025), oil on quilted fabric on panel, 79 x 51 inches
Morrison‚Äôs own memories and connections find their way into his collection of books, foods, photographs, and other items in an almost seek-and-find fashion. At first glance, the tableaux appear simply as collections of everyday things like vases, fruit, and cameras. But upon closer inspection, tiny visages appear along with references to dogs, from bones stitched into patchwork backgrounds to the sleepy face of a pooch in the center of a starfish and a bunch of green grapes with puppy faces. Always relaxed, even sleepy, the dogs‚Äô expressions evoke a calm sweetness, even nostalgia, paired with a sense of abundance.
In this series, the artist grapples with what belonging means, from revisiting his childhood home in Maine to thinking about his past decade in New York City to spending two months in Paris, where, ‚Äúdespite being married to a Frenchman, having many French friends, and having spent considerable time in the city, I had never felt at home,‚Äù he says. ‚ÄúThe ornate beauty of the architecture and the sense I have of everything being solidly ‚Äòin its place‚Äô makes it hard to feel inspired there for me.‚Äù So, he set out to explore that sense of disjointedness and creative conflict.
France is referenced in Morrison‚Äôs paintings by backgrounds of toile, or toile de jouy, a fabric design popular in the 18th century that features pastoral scenes, while Maine is represented by patchwork quilts he co-designed with his mother, who actually stitched them before they were incorporated into the works. ‚ÄúBy bringing the objects and backgrounds into my dog world, I‚Äôve rewritten my external material world through this lens, creating a new and more uniquely personal vision of these places,‚Äù he says.
Morrison will be an artist-in-residence at BUoY in Tokyo this summer, where he‚Äôs looking forward to incorporating Japanese textiles into a new series of paintings. He‚Äôs also preparing for a pop-up solo exhibition at Lazy Mike Gallery in Seoul and a group exhibition at Hashimoto Contemporary. Dog Show #5: Field Recordings continues through March 28 in New York. See more on the artist‚Äôs Instagram.
Detail of ‚Äú111 Limerock Street‚Äù
‚ÄúUntitled (Maine 2)‚Äù (2026), oil on quilted fabric on panel, 20 x 16 inches
‚ÄúUntitled (Paris 2)‚Äù (2025), oil on canvas, 20 x 15 inches
‚ÄúUntitled (NYC)‚Äù (2026), oil on panel, 20 x 16 inches
‚ÄúUntitled (Paris 1)‚Äù (2025), oil on canvas, 20 x 16 inches
‚ÄúUntitled (Maine 1)‚Äù (2026), oil on quilted fabric on panel, 20 x 16 inches
Detail of ‚ÄúUntitled (Maine 1)‚Äù

title: Is There an Ethical Path for AI Art?
description:
Feature
                There is a destabilizing, dreamlike sense of awe in encountering something without knowing the answer to
body:
Feature
                There is a destabilizing, dreamlike sense of awe in encountering something without knowing the answer to sanity‚Äôs most fundamental question: ‚ÄúIs this real?‚Äù
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Film still of Gr√©gory Chatonsky, "Completion 1.0" (2021) (image courtesy the artist)HOUSTON ‚Äî¬ÝIt‚Äôs perfectly normal to fear the AI apocalypse: the loss of jobs, the destruction of natural resources, the bastardization of human creativity for corporate profit, whether or not it will kill us all, etc., etc. Given the way that certain tech billionaires are behaving ‚Äî and how someone like Grimes, the tech-billionaire-adjacent artist and musician, has been talking lately ‚Äî it seems like it's become increasingly difficult for that class to notice the difference between a cool sci-fi thought experiment and mass human suffering. There is understandable fear among artists that artificial intelligence will plunder their work and render already-difficult careers impossible. This sets up the question: Is there an ethical path forward for art and AI?Perhaps my brain was addled by all the Texas sunshine, but a recent trip to the exhibition Imaging after Photography at Rice University in Houston has me convinced that the answer is yes ‚Äî artificial intelligence can be a force for real, exciting innovation in art when it is wielded by artists who engage with it critically. Inside the gallery‚Äôs glowing interior, I spoke with Alison Weaver, the executive director of Rice‚Äôs Moody Center for the Arts and the show‚Äôs co-curator. ‚ÄúTechnology is not neutral,‚Äù she argued, because it is developed by humans and reflects their biases. The problem with AI has been that ‚Äúcorporate interests have been out front, and more voices need to be centered in the conversation‚Äù ‚Äî namely, artists‚Äô voices. (This was refreshing to hear, since invoking the generosity of one‚Äôs corporate donors has become something of a museum ritual.)Detail of Sofia Crespo cyanotype from "Temporally Uncaptured" (2023‚Äì24)The exhibition is not a survey of art and artificial intelligence, but rather a snapshot of how seven contemporary artists have been working through AI‚Äôs implications for photography since 2020. As the title implies, Imaging after Photography argues that we are in a post-photographic moment, as artificial intelligence upends the everyday assumption that just because an image is photorealistic, it is also real. On the question of ethics, the artists in the show are careful to train the algorithms they use to generate their work on visual data sets of either their own images or works largely in the public domain. (Good for universities to set the standard here: no plagiarism!) Much of the art features images morphing or developing onscreen before your eyes, or grids that map a concept through repetition. The work ‚Äî perhaps owing to the moment or the medium, or both ‚Äî is iterative by nature.Installation view of Sofia Crespo, ‚ÄúTemporally Uncaptured‚Äù (2023‚Äì24)Interestingly, the exhibition‚Äôs most compelling pieces were by its lesser-known artists. The show really gets going in the second of its two galleries, as you step into a revolving door of speculative fictions: The darkened space is organized like a massive centrifuge, divided by luminescent rectangular screens. At the room‚Äôs center is a circular bench, giving the space an aptly dizzying, panopticon-like effect. As a lover of analog photography, I was immediately pulled to Sofia Crespo‚Äôs slice of the room, where the screens displayed the botanist and photographer Anna Atkins‚Äôs famous 19th-century cyanotypes of British algae. Crespo, an Argentine artist based in Portugal, works at the intersection of AI and biological systems, and used Atkins‚Äôs work as a data set for imagining single- and multi-cellular organism development. The resulting images of fantastical specimens, reproduced in morphological grids, are wonderfully mystifying: exoskeletons, mandibles, cell walls, cilia, placentae, all printed by Crespo using Atkins‚Äôs old-school cyanotype process in exquisite blue detail.Detail of Nouf Aljowaysir, ‚ÄúMachine Vision Research in Archival Images‚Äù (2020)A similarly sci-fi approach unfolds in the work of Joan Fontcuberta. His series What Darwin Missed (2024) likewise references a pivotal moment in the 19th-century history of science, using photographs of corals the artist took on diving trips to the Galapagos Islands ‚Äî where Charles Darwin developed his theory of evolution ‚Äî as the basis for AI images of imagined species. Fontcuberta then allegedly hangs these AI creatures alongside his actual photographs of coral in the exhibition; I use the word ‚Äúallegedly‚Äù because it is genuinely impossible to tell what is real and what isn‚Äôt, which prompts one to distrust everything. I even began to eye with suspicion the ornate specimen of black coral standing in a glass case nearby, which was in fact on loan from the Houston Museum of Natural Science. There is a destabilizing, dreamlike sense of awe in encountering something without knowing the answer to sanity‚Äôs most fundamental question: ‚ÄúIs this real?‚ÄùThe denial of such speculative fantasies is equally thought-provoking, as we see in the work of Nouf Aljowaysir, a Saudi Arabian-born artist living in Brooklyn. Aljowaysir transforms 19th-century orientalist photographs of West Asia‚Äî images laden with imperialist violence, as Europeans forcibly documented the peoples they encountered on colonial missions ‚Äî using artificial intelligence, creating empty voids where the figures once stood, perhaps a nod to √âdouard Glissant‚Äôs call for a decolonial ‚Äúright to opacity.‚Äù On the opposite wall, she simultaneously shows AI‚Äôs failure to comprehend Arab subjects: the algorithm labels a Bedouin home a bunker and civilians as military combatants, camels are classified as horses (an obvious regional bias), and empty land becomes an airport. As the US military seeks to use similar technology to rain firepower down on Iran, killing schoolchildren, the ongoing implications of these systems are clear.Installation view of Gr√©gory Chatonsky, "Completion 1.0" (2021); all other photos Julia Curl/Hyperallergic)That so much of the exhibition looks back at the mid-19th century is worth probing. As the first Industrial Revolution came to a close and the second one ramped up, much of the world had been mapped, photographed, or catalogued in some form. Pockets of the unknown remained ‚Äî the Amazon, the ocean‚Äôs depths, the Arctic and Antarctic poles ‚Äî and artistic and literary imaginations ran wild speculating about (and perhaps yearning for) their undiscovered wonders. The Western world abounded in speculative fictions about cannibalistic plants, monstrous sea creatures, and the poles as portals to a hollow earth. At the same time, the documentary voyages to these places were colonial, extractive missions, capturing peoples and places that Western empires sought to control. Now, as climate change decimates the environment and life is increasingly subject to 24/7 digital surveillance, perhaps art‚Äôs first forays into artificial intelligence evoke a parallel desire for that which we have lost today: the fearsome agency of an unknowable world.Editor's note: Travel and accommodations for the author were paid for by the Moody Center for the Arts at Rice University.
